elements.sent-2411-001: We report an anomalous temperature-induced transition in thermal conductivity in germanene monolayer around a critical temperature $T_c = 350 \, \text{K}$.
elements.sent-2411-002: Equilibrium molecular dynamics simulations reveal a transition from $\kappa \sim T^{-2}$ scaling below $T_c$ to $\kappa \sim T^{-1/2}$ above, contrasting with conventional $\kappa \sim T^{-1}$ behavior.
elements.sent-2411-003: This anomalous scaling correlates with long-scale characteristics timescale $\tau_2$ obtained from double exponential fitting of heat current autocorrelation function.
elements.sent-2411-004: Phonon mode analysis using normal mode decomposition indicates that a redshift in TA phonons reduces the acoustic-optical phonon gap, enhancing the phonon-phonon scattering and driving the anomalous scaling behavior.
elements.sent-2411-005: Moreover, non equilibrium simulations find a convergent thermal conductivity of germanene with sample size, in agreement with mode coupling theory, owing to high scattering of ZA phonons due to the inherent buckling of germanene.
elements.sent-2411-006: Introduction\protect Germanene, the two-dimensional (2D) analog of germanium, is an experimentally realized distinctive 2D material with promising electronic and thermal properties \cite{Yuhara2020, Acun2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Composed of a single atomic layer of Ge atoms in a hexagonal lattice \cite{Roome2014 , germanene differs fundamentally from planar 2D materials like graphene, h-BN etc., due to its intrinsic buckling which introduces out-of-plane atomic displacements \cite{Cahangirov2009 .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: This inherent buckling not only enables tunable properties \cite{ARJMAND2018657 as a result of additional degrees of freedom but also enhances scattering of out-of-plane (flexural) phonons, a feature with significant implications for germanene’s thermal transport behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Several studies have focused on understanding the electronic properties of germanene \cite{Li2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The electrical and thermal conductivity have been previously studied by Chegel et al. \cite{Chegel2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: It is found that the band gap of germanene can be opened and increased to 20-310 meV \cite{Ye2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Application of germanene is explored in case of Li and Na ion batteries resulting in high charge capacities \cite{Mortazavi2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Electronic properties due to external defects are found to be be highly sensitive with vanishing Dirac cone \cite{Padilha2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Similarly, functioanlization with H, I, F, Cl and Br is proposed for topological insulating device based on germanene \cite{Si2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The thermal transported of germanene is reported in a few works, compared to the extensive studies based on electronic properties.
elements.sent-2411-016: Previous work on the thermal properties of germanene reports a low thermal conductivity, attributed to its intrinsic buckling and relatively high atomic mass.
elements.sent-2411-017: Peng et al. \cite{Peng2016prb reported a thermal conductivity of 2.4 W/(m K) at 300 K using the Boltzmann transport equation with ab-initio force constants, while Das et al. \cite{das2019 found 3.1 W/(m K) using the Debye-Callaway model.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: These values contrast sharply with planar 2D materials, supporting the hypothesis that buckling reduces thermal transport efficiency \cite{Tang2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: External strain, however, significantly enhances thermal conductivity, with pronounced size effects under increased strain \cite{Kuang2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Silicene-germanene superlattices also show a non-monotonic thermal conductivity due to phonon confinement effects \cite{WangNano2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Vibrational studies indicate that specific heat in germanene follows a $T^2$ dependency at low temperatures, further highlighting its unique thermal characteristics \cite{Zaveh2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: While the thermal transport properties of graphene have been extensively studied \cite{nika2017phonons, Balandin2008, lindsay2014phonon , systematic investigations into buckled monolayer germanene are limited.}
elements.sent-2411-023: An in-depth understanding of phonon propagation and thermal conductivity in such 2D materials is crucial for their optimization in high-performance applications.
elements.sent-2411-024: To date, however, the literature lacks comprehensive experimental data and theoretical analyses on thermal transport in germanene, particularly on the role of temperature induced phonon relaxation effects.
elements.sent-2411-025: This study seeks to fill this gap, presenting detailed analyses of germanene’s temperature-dependent phonon thermal transport and its implications for future applications.
elements.sent-2411-026: This paper is organized as follows: Sec.
elements.sent-2411-027: Computational Details\protect We use the Large-scale Atomic/Molecular Massively Parallel Simulator (Lammps) \cite{lammps for carrying out the molecular dynamics simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The verlet algorithm is used for integrating Newton's equation of motion.
elements.sent-2411-029: The modified Stillinger-Weber (SW) potential is used for modeling the interatomic interactions.
elements.sent-2411-030: We take the optimized parameters of SW potential for all the simulations from the work of Jiang et al \cite{Jiang2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: It is noteworthy that the among available choices of potentials, Tersoff potential is unsuitable for our calculations as the sheets deform at 700 K \cite{Thapliyal2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The periodic boundary condition has been applied in all three directions.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, to make the out-of-plane direction of germanene monolayer aperiodic, we have added 50 {\AA vacuum in the Z-direction.}
elements.sent-2411-034: To obtained optimized geometry molecular mechanics based minimization with steepest descent and conjugate gradient are carried out with with a tolerance of 10$^{-14}$ for energy and 10$^{-14}$ eV/{\AA for force.}
elements.sent-2411-035: It is noteworthy that bond order Tersoff potential leads to unstable geometry at the high temperature regime, so SW potential must be chosen to simulate the systems \cite{Thapliyal2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: To equilibrate the systems for thermal conductivity calculations, we have simulated the system in isothermal-isobaric (NPT) ensemble and thereafter in isothermal (NVT) ensemble for 1 ns respectively (Fig. S1, S2 in SI) with 1 fs timestep for integrating Newton's equation of motion.
elements.sent-2411-037: We use the Nose-Hoover thermostat with coupling constant of 0.1 ps and Nose-Hoover barostat with 1 ps coupling constant for maintaining temperature (100-950 K) and pressure (0 bar) throughout simulation runs.
elements.sent-2411-038: We calculate the thermal conductivity by ensemble averaging 30 independent trajectories, created by randomizing the velocity distribution of Ge atoms.
elements.sent-2411-039: Each trajectory is simulated for an 2 ns with fine timestep of 0.1 fs in NVT ensemble (Fig. S3 in SI).
elements.sent-2411-040: In equilibrium formalism, the thermal conductivity ($\kappa$) at a ambient temperature T is calculated by using Green-Kubo relation by integrating the heat current auto-correlation function: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:gk_cond} \bm{\kappa}(T) = \frac{1}{Vk_BT^2}\int_{0}^{\infty} \langle \bm{S}(0).\bm{S}(t) \rangle dt.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-041: Here, the volume is calculated as V=$L_x\times L_y\times$4.24, where $L_x$ and $L_y$ are the dimension of the simulation cell in X and Y direction and the van der Waals radius of Ge atoms is taken as the thickness.
elements.sent-2411-042: The heat current operator, S(t) is calculated by: \begin{equation}S(t) = \sum_i E_iv_i+\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i<j}(f_{ij}.(v_i+v_j))r_{ij},\end{equation} with the force between $i^{th}$ and $j^{th}$ atom given by $f_{ij}$, $E_i$ is the energy, $v_i$ is the velocity of $i^{th}$ atom and $r_{ij}$ is the corresponding displacement vector.
elements.sent-2411-043: More details on the calculations of phonon density of state (PDOS), dispersion relation are available in our previous works \cite{santosh2018,sapta2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: We use the Fourier's law to calculate the thermal conductivity from the non-equilibrium simulations, given by: \begin{equation}J=-\kappa\nabla T,\end{equation} where J is the heatflux flowing from hotter to colder regions of the simulation.
elements.sent-2411-045: Unlike in case of equilibrium simulations, for simulating the systems in non equilibrium condition, we fix one end of the sheet to a higher temperature of 320 K and the other end of the system at 280 K by using Langevin thermostat, as implemented in Lammps.
elements.sent-2411-046: The phonon power spectrum is calculated from the velocity auto-correlation function by \cite{dynaphopy : \begin{equation}G_{\bm{q}j}(\omega)= 2\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \langle v_{\bm{q}j}^*(0)v_{\bm{q}j}(\tau)\rangle e^{i\omega\tau}d\tau,\end{equation} where, the velocity autocorrelation function is: \begin{equation}\langle v_{\bm{q}j}^*(0)v_{\bm{q}j}(\tau)\rangle = \lim_{t\prime\rightarrow\infty}\frac{1}{t\prime}\int_{0}^{t\prime}v_{\bm{q}j}^*(t+\tau)v_{\bm{q}j}(t)dt.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Here, $v_{\bm{q j}$ is the velocity of phonon branch $j$ corresponding to a wavevector $\bm{q}$, obtained from MD simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-048: We use the normal mode decomposition technique to project atomic velocities on the wavevector $\bm{q}$: \begin{equation}\bm{v}^{\bm{q}}_l(t)= \sqrt{\frac{m_l}{N}}\sum_{k}e^{-i\bm{q}.\bm{r}_{kl}^0}\bm{v}_{kl}(t).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-049: Projecting into phonon eigenvector $e(q, j)$, we have: \begin{equation}v_{\bm{q}j}(t) = \sum_lv_l^{\bm{q}}(t).e_l^*(\bm{q},j).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-050: Now, the power spectrum is fitted with a Lorentzian to obtain the phonon linewidth $\gamma_{\bm{q j}$ as: \begin{equation}G_{\bm{q}j}\equiv\frac{\langle \left| v_{\bm{q}j}(t)\right|^2\rangle}{\frac{1}{2}\gamma_{\bm{q}j}\pi\left(1+\left(\frac{\omega-\widetilde{\omega}_{\bm{q}j}}{\frac{1}{2}\gamma_{\bm{q}j}}\right)^2\right)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Here, $\omega$ is the frequency at peak, and $\gamma$ represent the half width at half maximum.
elements.sent-2411-052: The phonon lifetime is calculated by $\tau = 1/(2\gamma)$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Effect of Temperature on Structural Geometry.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[height=6.5cm,keepaspectratio]{fig_1} \caption{\label{fig:structure}(a) The lattice constant and (b) buckling height of a germanene monolayer as functions of temperature. Both lattice constant and buckling height decrease monotonically, with a rapid decline observed between 300 K and 400 K.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Germanene, comprising a hexagonal lattice of Ge atoms, exhibits intrinsic buckling that distinguishes it from planar 2D materials like graphene and h-BN.
elements.sent-2411-056: All structural parameters are based on the experimentally reported geometry \cite{expt-str .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Molecular mechanics optimization yields an lattice constant of $a =3.98$ \AA~and a buckling height of 0.71 \AA, with the system maintaining the symmetry operations of the P-3m1 space group.
elements.sent-2411-058: A 32$\times$32$\times$1 supercell containing 2048 Ge atoms is used for all simulations.
elements.sent-2411-059: To investigate the thermal effects on the lattice geometry, we conduct simulations at a fixed pressure of 0 bar in NPT ensemble across a range of temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-060: Figure \ref{fig:structure (a,b) illustrates the temperature dependence of the lattice constant and buckling height.}
elements.sent-2411-061: We observe a monotonic decrease in both parameters with increasing temperature, though their rates of change vary across different temperature regimes.
elements.sent-2411-062: At lower temperatures, the lattice constant remains nearly constant, while buckling height decreases.
elements.sent-2411-063: In the intermediate range (300-400 K), both lattice constant and buckling height show a more rapid decline at comparable rates.
elements.sent-2411-064: Beyond 400 K, however, the lattice constant stabilizes, while the buckling height continues to decrease at a slightly faster rate.
elements.sent-2411-065: This temperature-dependent behavior reflects a dual mechanism of structural relaxation.
elements.sent-2411-066: Initially, as thermal energy increases, germanene primarily relaxes through reductions in buckling height, which equilibrate internal stresses without significantly altering the in-plane lattice.
elements.sent-2411-067: Around 300 K, further temperature increases lead to a cooperative relaxation between lattice constant contraction and buckling height reduction.
elements.sent-2411-068: As temperature increases beyond 400 K, the competing relaxations stabilize, with the lattice constant decreasing at a slower rate, and buckling remaining the dominant mode of structural adaptation.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[height=6.5cm,keepaspectratio]{fig_2} \caption{\label{fig:kappavsT} Thermal conductivity of a germanene monolayer as a function of temperature. An anomalous transition in $\kappa$ is observed near $T_c\sim$ 350 K, with distinct power-law relations with exponents -2 and -1/2 below and above $T_c$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-070: The thermal conductivity of a germanene monolayer is determined using equilibrium molecular dynamics simulations, yielding a value of $3.6 \pm 0.4 \, \text{W/m.K}$ at room temperature, in close agreement with prior studies \cite{Thapliyal2024,Peng2016prb, das2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-071: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:kappavsT , $\kappa$ demonstrates a clear, monotonic decline with increasing temperature, typical of enhanced phonon-phonon scattering that limits heat transport at higher temperatures.}
elements.sent-2411-072: However, a distinct and unexpected transition in thermal conductivity behavior occurs at a critical temperature $T_c \approx 350$ K, where the temperature scaling of $\kappa$ markedly shifts, revealing unique thermal transport characteristics in germanene not observed in most 2D materials.
elements.sent-2411-073: This transition is quantified by power-law fits to the data below and above $T_c$, showing that $\kappa \sim T^{-2}$ for temperatures below $T_c$ and $\kappa \sim T^{-1/2}$ for temperatures above $T_c$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Such behavior deviates from the conventional $\kappa \sim T^{-1}$ trend typical for crystalline 2D systems, \cite{Qin2017, Carruthers1961 where phonon scattering rates are expected to yield a single scaling regime.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The observed $T^{-2}$ scaling below $T_c$ indicates an unusually rapid decay in thermal conductivity with temperature, suggesting that lower-temperature heat transport in germanene is highly sensitive to the interactions between specific phonon modes, while higher temperatures favor a reduced sensitivity, as evidenced by the more gradual $T^{-1/2}$ scaling above $T_c$.
elements.sent-2411-076: The observed shift from $T^{-2}$ to $T^{-1/2}$ scaling at $T_c$ highlights a critical threshold in the thermal behavior of germanene, demarcating two distinct heat transport regimes.
elements.sent-2411-077: Below $T_c$, enhanced thermal resistivity dominates due to specific phonon scattering interactions, while above $T_c$, these processes diminish or reconfigure, leading to a more stable thermal regime.
elements.sent-2411-078: This anomalous transition is likely tied to the unique buckled structure of germanene, offering key insights into designing germanene-based devices.
elements.sent-2411-079: In the following, we explore these mechanisms by analyzing the relaxation phenomena and phonon dispersion, density of states, and linewidths.
elements.sent-2411-080: Mechanisms Underlying Phonon Thermal Transport To investigate the underlying heat transport mechanisms in germanene, we analyze the nature of heat current autocorrelation function (HCACF) through double-exponential fitting: \begin{equation}\frac{\langle \bm{S}(0) \cdot \bm{S}(t) \rangle}{\langle \bm{S}(0) \cdot \bm{S}(0) \rangle} = \alpha_0 e^{-t/\tau_1} + (1 - \alpha_0) e^{-t/\tau_2},\end{equation} where $\tau_1$ is the short range characteristic timescale, $\tau_2$ is the long range characteristic timescale, and $\alpha_0$ (0 $\le \alpha_0 \le$ 1) is a fitting parameter.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\frac{\langle S(0) \cdot S(t) \rangle}{\langle S(0) \cdot S(0) \rangle} = \alpha_0 e^{-t/\tau_1} + (1 - \alpha_0) e^{-t/\tau_2},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-081: Figure \ref{fig:tauvsT (a) shows the temperature dependence of $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The slow decay characteristic timescale $\tau_1$ decreases up to the transition temperature and then begins to increase, while $\tau_2$ decreases monotonically.
elements.sent-2411-083: While $\tau_1$ shows no clear monotonic trend with $T$, $\tau_2$ demonstrates a temperature-dependent pattern that is qualitatively analogous to the behavior of $\kappa$.
elements.sent-2411-084: A power-law fit yields $\tau_2 \sim T^{-2.04}$ at lower temperatures below $T_c$, closely mirroring the thermal conductivity behavior, and $\tau_2 \sim T^{-0.57}$ at higher temperatures above $T_c$.
elements.sent-2411-085: In Figure \ref{fig:tauvsT (b), thermal conductivity is plotted against $\tau_2$, showing a clear linear relationship that directly links the long-scale characteristic time to thermal transport.}
elements.sent-2411-086: This strong correlation supports the central role of $\tau_2$ in governing the temperature-dependent thermal conductivity.
elements.sent-2411-087: While the double-exponential model elucidates the connection between thermal conductivity and HCACF decay, mode-dependent phonon analysis is required for a more comprehensive understanding.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[height=6.5cm,keepaspectratio]{fig_3} \caption{\label{fig:tauvsT} (a) Scaled long-scale (circles) and short-scale (triangles) characteristic times with temperature for germanene monolayer, derived from double-exponential HCACF fitting, and (b) thermal conductivity as a function of the long-scale characteristic time. The long-scale characteristic time follows the same temperature dependence as thermal conductivity, while the short-scale characteristic time dips near the transition temperature.}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[height=6.5cm,keepaspectratio]{fig_4} \caption{\label{fig:dispersion} (a) Phonon dispersion and density of states for germanene along $\Gamma\rightarrow \text{K} \rightarrow \text{M}\rightarrow\Gamma$ direction. (b-d) Angular frequency deviation from harmonic approximation with temperature at high-symmetry $\Gamma$, M, and K points. Notably, the acoustic gap narrows with temperature, contributing to thermal conductivity reduction.}
elements.sent-2411-088: To explore further the thermal transport mechanisms in monolayer germanene, we analyze the phonon density of states (DOS) and dispersion along the $\Gamma \rightarrow \text{K \rightarrow \text{M \rightarrow \Gamma$ path (Fig. \ref{fig:dispersion (a)).}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: The maximum LO/TO frequency of 14.1 THz aligns with Jiang et al. \cite{Jiang2017 , and the absence of negative frequencies supports structural stability at simulated temperatures.}
elements.sent-2411-090: As expected for 2D materials, the ZA mode exhibits quadratic dispersion, while TA and LA modes show cubic behavior \cite{sapta_beryllene, Gu2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-091: The DOS peak at 13.13 THz, dominated by LO phonons, undergoes a redshift with temperature, consistent with similar trends observed in other 2D materials \cite{santosh2018, sapta2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-092: To assess temperature effects on phonon dispersion, we plot frequency deviations at high-symmetry points in Fig. \ref{fig:dispersion (b-d).}
elements.sent-2411-093: At $\Gamma$ (Fig. \ref{fig:dispersion (b)), acoustic modes remain unchanged due to symmetry, though the ZO mode shows significant redshift, reducing the acoustic-optical (AO) gap.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Both TO and LO frequencies decrease modestly with temperature up to 650 K, after which redshifting intensifies.
elements.sent-2411-095: At the M point (Fig. \ref{fig:dispersion (c)), the ZA and TA modes experience abrupt decrease in frequency around the transition temperature (350 K), with the TA mode exhibiting particularly strong redshifting.}
elements.sent-2411-096: While ZO modes show frequency reductions across temperatures, minimal shifts in LA modes further compress the AO gap.
elements.sent-2411-097: At the K point, (Fig. \ref{fig:dispersion (d)) te ZA modes show significant redshifting near the transition temperature, while the ZO/TO and LA modes are slightly decreased, leading to a decreased AO gap.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Overall, the AO gap narrowing with temperature significantly impacts thermal conductivity, similar to the observations in other 2D materials \cite{Lindsay2013, Gu2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-099: The sharp AO gap reduction at M point likely correlates with the anomalous thermal conductivity transition.
elements.sent-2411-100: In Fig. \ref{fig:linewidth (a), we present the linewidth of different phonon branches with phonon angular frequency at the room temperature.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The acoustic branches, primarily responsible for thermal transport are found to posses linewidths than optical modes.
elements.sent-2411-102: Intriguingly, unlike planar 2D materials like graphene, where the ZA mode displays suppressed linewidth, the out-of-plane and in-plane acoustic modes in germanene exhibit comparable linewidths at 300 K.
elements.sent-2411-103: The temperature-dependent phonon linewidths are plotted for $\Gamma$, M, and K points in Fig. \ref{fig:linewidth (b-d).}
elements.sent-2411-104: Linewidths increase with temperature, reflecting intensified phonon scattering at elevated temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-105: At $\Gamma$, optical phonon linewidths, particularly ZO, rise sharply near 350 K (Fig. \ref{fig:linewidth (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-106: The acoustic modes show significant increase in linewidth at the M point.
elements.sent-2411-107: From Fig. \ref{fig:linewidth (c), it is evident that the linewidth of ZA and TA increase significantly at this symmetry point.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Moreover, a clear transition is observed for TA and ZA phonons near the transition temperature.
elements.sent-2411-109: This rapid increase in linewidth reduces the phonon lifetime, thus reducing the thermal conductivity significantly.
elements.sent-2411-110: Similarly, at the K point the ZA/TA acoustic modes show rapid increase in linewidth near 350 K (Fig. \ref{fig:linewidth (d)).}
elements.sent-2411-111: This pronounced followed by a steady increases of acoustic phonon linewidths underpin the observed thermal conductivity transition.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[height=6.5cm,keepaspectratio]{fig_5} \caption{\label{fig:linewidth} (a) Mode-specific phonon linewidth of germanene at room temperature as a function of angular frequency. Phonon linewidth variation with temperature at (b) $\Gamma$, (c) M, and (d) K points. Linewidth increases with temperature, reducing phonon lifetimes and thermal conductivity.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[height=6.5cm,keepaspectratio]{fig_6} \caption{\label{fig:kappavsL} Thermal conductivity of a germanene monolayer as a function of sample length, calculated using NEMD simulations at room temperature and scaled to thermal conductivity at a 1 $\mu$m sample length. A convergence in thermal conductivity is observed due to pronounced out-of-plane scattering.}
elements.sent-2411-113: In low-dimensional materials, thermal conductivity often exhibits a pronounced dependence on sample dimensions, challenging the assumption of intrinsic behavior.
elements.sent-2411-114: In such cases, it is important to investigate the effect of sample length on the thermal transport in these materials.
elements.sent-2411-115: Non equilibrium based approach is suitable for understanding the length dependence of thermal conductivity as the approach can simulate a microdevice measurement on sample placed in between hot and cold regions (Fig. S4 in SI) \cite{Gu2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: In Fig. \ref{fig:kappavsL , we present the thermal conductivity of monolayer germanene as a function of sample length, calculated at 300 K.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Thermal conductivity increases consistently with length up to 500 nm before saturating at approximately 1 $\mu$m.
elements.sent-2411-118: According to mode coupling theory, the behavior of the HCACF can indicate convergence or divergence in thermal conductivity, with $t^{-1}$ scaling signifying divergence and $t^{-1.5}$ or faster indicating convergence \cite{Lepri2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-119: Here, the scaled HCACF exhibits a decay of $t^{-1.6}$ (Fig. S5 in SI), indicating convergent thermal conductivity.
elements.sent-2411-120: This convergence is attributed to the reduced contribution from flexural phonons, influenced by germanene's inherent buckling.
elements.sent-2411-121: Fig. \ref{fig:linewidth (a) shows that the out-of-plane and in-plane acoustic phonon branches exhibit similar linewidths due to symmetry breaking caused by buckling.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Consequently, the ZA phonons contribute less to the overall thermal conductivity, leading to convergence at larger sample sizes.
elements.sent-2411-123: Conclusions\protect In conclusion, we have systematically investigated the thermal conductivity mechanism of monolayer germanene using molecular dynamics simulations within the Green-Kubo formalism across an extensive temperature range, employing an optimized Stillinger-Weber potential parameter set.
elements.sent-2411-124: We report an anomalous temperature-induced transition in the thermal conductivity of germanene monolayer at a critical temperature of 350 K.
elements.sent-2411-125: Our analysis reveal that the thermal conductivity follows an unusual scaling: $\kappa \sim T^{-2}$ below $T_c$, transitioning to $\kappa \sim T^{-1/2}$ above $T_c$, a stark departure from the typical $\kappa \sim T^{-1}$ trend.
elements.sent-2411-126: This anomalous scaling is closely mirrored in the slow relaxation timescale $\tau_2$ of the HCACF, with $\tau_2 \sim T^{-2}$ below $T_c$ and $\tau_2 \sim T^{-1/2}$ above, confirming the direct relationship $\kappa \sim \tau_2$.
elements.sent-2411-127: Phonon analysis further elucidates this behavior, with a significant reduction in the AO gap, primarily due to redshifting of the TA phonon modes at high-symmetry M and K points.
elements.sent-2411-128: This redshifting, coupled with a rapid increase in linewidths near $T_c$, signifies intensified phonon scattering, fundamentally altering the thermal transport pathways.
elements.sent-2411-129: Additionally, NEMD simulations indicate that the thermal conductivity of germanene converges with sample length, a characteristic attributed to comparable contributions from both in-plane and out-of-plane phonon modes, consistent with mode-coupling theory.
elements.sent-2411-130: This anomalous scaling correlates with the long-scale characteristic timescale $\tau_2$ obtained from double exponential fitting of the heat current autocorrelation function.
elements.sent-2411-131: Phonon mode analysis using normal mode decomposition indicates that a redshift in ZO phonons reduces the acoustic-optical phonon gap, causing an overlap, enhances the phonon-phonon scattering, driving the anomalous scaling behavior.
elements.sent-2411-132: Moreover, nonequilibrium simulations find a convergent thermal conductivity of germanene with sample size, in agreement with mode coupling theory, owing to the high scattering of ZA phonons due to the inherent buckling of germanene.
elements.sent-2411-133: Composed of a single atomic layer of Ge atoms in a hexagonal lattice \cite{Roome2014 , germanene differs fundamentally from planar 2D materials like graphene, h-BN, etc., due to its intrinsic buckling, which introduces out-of-plane atomic displacements \cite{Cahangirov2009 .}}
elements.sent-2411-134: This inherent buckling not only enables tunable properties \cite{ARJMAND2018657 as a result of additional degrees of freedom but also enhances scattering of out-of-plane (flexural) phonons, a feature with significant implications in the thermal transport of germanene.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Several studies have focused on understanding the electronic properties of germanene \cite{Li2014_nano .}
elements.sent-2411-136: The application of germanene is explored in the case of Li and Na ion batteries, resulting in high charge capacities \cite{Mortazavi2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-137: Electronic properties due to external defects are found to be highly sensitive to vanishing Dirac cone \cite{Padilha2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-138: Similarly, functionalization with H, I, F, Cl, and Br is proposed for the topological insulating devices based on germanene \cite{Si2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-139: The thermal transport of germanene is reported in a few works, compared to the extensive studies based on electronic properties.
elements.sent-2411-140: To date, the literature lacks comprehensive experimental data and theoretical analyses on thermal transport in germanene, particularly on the role of temperature-induced phonon relaxation effects.
elements.sent-2411-141: The Verlet algorithm is used for integrating Newton's equation of motion.
elements.sent-2411-142: A lot of available potentials for germanene monolayer, including the machine learning potentials, are compared in the literature for accuracy in classical simulations.
elements.sent-2411-143: Among the potentials studied, Tersoff, Modified Embedded Atom Method (MEAM), and Stillinger-Weber (SW) are recommended for simulating germanene \cite{Marcin2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-144: However, Tersoff potential is unsuitable for our calculations as the systems simulated using this potential deform at 700 K \cite{Thapliyal2024 , which is inconsistent with the literature \cite{Peng2016prb .}}
elements.sent-2411-145: So, the modified SW potential is used for modeling the interatomic interactions.
elements.sent-2411-146: However, to make the out-of-plane direction of the germanene monolayer aperiodic, we have added a 50 {\AA vacuum in the Z-direction.}
elements.sent-2411-147: To obtain optimized geometry, molecular mechanics-based minimizations with the steepest descent and the conjugate gradient algorithms are carried out with a tolerance of 10$^{-14}$ for normalized energy difference and 10$^{-14}$ eV/{\AA for force.}
elements.sent-2411-148: To equilibrate the systems for thermal conductivity calculations, we have simulated the system in isothermal-isobaric (NPT) ensemble and thereafter in an isothermal (NVT) ensemble for 500 ps respectively (Fig. S1, S2 in SI) with 1 fs timestep for integrating Newton's equation of motion.
elements.sent-2411-149: We use the Nose-Hoover thermostat with a coupling constant of 0.1 ps and Nose-Hoover barostat with 1 ps coupling constant for maintaining temperature (100-950 K) and pressure (0 bar) throughout simulation runs.
elements.sent-2411-150: Each trajectory is simulated for 2 ns in NVT ensemble (Fig. S3 in SI) with a fine timestep of 0.1 fs, which is more than sufficient to resolve the highest phonon frequency of the germanene monolayer \cite{Wang2015, Pei2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-151: In equilibrium formalism, the thermal conductivity ($\kappa$) at an ambient temperature T is calculated by using the Green-Kubo relation by integrating the heat current auto-correlation function: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:gk_cond} \bm{\kappa}(T) = \frac{1}{Vk_BT^2}\int_{0}^{\infty} \langle \bm{S}(0).\bm{S}(t) \rangle dt.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-152: Here, the volume is calculated as V=$L_x\times L_y\times$(4.24 \AA), where $L_x$ and $L_y$ are the dimensions of the simulation cell in the X and Y directions, and the van der Waals radius of Ge atoms (4.24 \AA) is taken as the thickness \cite{Mantina2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-153: The temperature variation of thermal conductivity for the germanene monolayer is calculated using this method.
elements.sent-2411-154: The heat current operator, S(t) is calculated by: \begin{equation}S(t) = \sum_i E_iv_i+\sum_{i}\sigma_iv_i,\end{equation} with $E_i$ is the energy, $v_i$ is the velocity of $i^{th}$ atom, and $\sigma_i$ represents the virial stress tensor calculated from both the two-body and three body term of the potentail \cite{Surblys2019, Surblys2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-155: More details on the calculations of phonon density of state (PDOS) and dispersion relations are available in our previous works \cite{santosh2018,sapta2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-156: We use Fourier's law to calculate the thermal conductivity from the nonequilibrium simulations, given by: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:fourier} J=-\kappa\nabla T,\end{equation} where J is the heatflux flowing from hotter to colder regions of the simulation.. \cite{Mantina2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-157: The sample length variation of thermal conductivity for the germanene monolayer is calculated using this non-equilibrium-based method.
elements.sent-2411-158: We have used systems of length 10 nm to 10000 nm for the calculation of the length dependence of thermal conductivity.
elements.sent-2411-159: To achieve this, we have created 25$\times$25$\times$1 to 25000$\times$25$\times$1 supercells consisting of 1250 atoms to 1250000 atoms.
elements.sent-2411-160: The same equilibration protocol is followed as explained earlier.
elements.sent-2411-161: Unlike in the case of equilibrium simulations, for simulating the systems in non-equilibrium conditions, we fix one end of the sheet to a higher temperature of 320 K and the other end of the system at 280 K (Fig. S4) by using a Langevin thermostat, as implemented in LAMMPS.
elements.sent-2411-162: A total of 5 ns NVE run with 1 fs integration timestep is done; out of which the last 1.5 ns is used to calculate the average temperature and heatflux in a block of 10 {\AA length.}
elements.sent-2411-163: The phonon power spectrum is calculated from the velocity auto-correlation function by \cite{dynaphopy : \begin{equation}G_{\bm{q}j}(\omega)= 2\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \langle v_{\bm{q}j}^*(0)v_{\bm{q}j}(\tau)\rangle e^{i\omega\tau}d\tau,\end{equation} where the velocity autocorrelation function is: \begin{equation}\langle v_{\bm{q}j}^*(0)v_{\bm{q}j}(\tau)\rangle = \lim_{t\prime\rightarrow\infty}\frac{1}{t\prime}\int_{0}^{t\prime}v_{\bm{q}j}^*(t+\tau)v_{\bm{q}j}(t)dt.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Here, $\omega$ is the frequency at peak, and $\gamma$ represents the half-width at half maximum.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[height=6.5cm,keepaspectratio]{fig_1} \caption{\label{fig:structure}(a) The lattice constant and (b) buckling height of a germanene monolayer as functions of temperature. The lattice constant and buckling height decrease monotonically, with a relatively rapid decline observed between 300 K and 400 K.}
elements.sent-2411-165: All structural parameters are based on the experimentally reported geometry \cite{expt-str, Yuhara2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-166: Molecular mechanics optimization yields a lattice constant of $a =3.98$ \AA~and a buckling height of 0.71 \AA, with the system maintaining the symmetry operations of the P-3m1 space group.
elements.sent-2411-167: This is sufficient for the calculation of thermal conductivity using MD simulations in buckled materials \cite{Li2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-168: To investigate the thermal effects on the lattice geometry, we conduct simulations at a fixed pressure of 0 bar in the NPT ensemble across a range of temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-169: This atypical behaviour of monotonic decrease of both the lattice constant and the buckling height has been previously observed in plumbene, a group IV 2D material like germanene \cite{Mohammadi2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-170: At lower temperatures, the lattice constant remains nearly constant, while the buckling height decreases.
elements.sent-2411-171: In the intermediate range (300-400 K), lattice constant and buckling height show a more rapid decline at comparable rates.
elements.sent-2411-172: Initially, as thermal energy increases, germanene primarily relaxes through reductions in buckling height, which equilibrates internal stresses without significantly altering the in-plane lattice.
elements.sent-2411-173: As temperature increases beyond 400 K, the competing relaxations stabilize, with the lattice constant decreasing at a slower rate and buckling remaining the dominant mode of structural adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-174: The thermal conductivity of a germanene monolayer is determined using equilibrium molecular dynamics simulations (Eq. \ref{eqn:gk_cond ), yielding a value of $3.6 \pm 0.4 \, \text{W/m.K}$ at room temperature, in close agreement with prior studies \cite{Thapliyal2024,Peng2016prb, das2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-175: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:kappavsT , $\kappa$ demonstrates a monotonic decline with increasing temperature, typical of enhanced phonon-phonon scattering that limits heat transport at higher temperatures.}
elements.sent-2411-176: The observed $T^{-2}$ scaling below $T_c$ indicates an unusually rapid decay in thermal conductivity with temperature, suggesting that lower-temperature heat transport in germanene is highly sensitive to the interactions between specific phonon modes.
elements.sent-2411-177: In contrast, higher temperatures favor a reduced sensitivity, as evidenced by the more gradual $T^{-1/2}$ scaling above $T_c$.
elements.sent-2411-178: It must be noted that no reliable quantum correction exists for classical thermal conductivity calculated from MD simulations \cite{Fan2017prb, Turney2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-179: As a result, we do not consider the quantum corrections \cite{Turney2017 in this work.}
elements.sent-2411-180: In graphene, thermal conductivity is underestimated by 10\% in classical calculations below the Debye temperature \cite{Fan2017prb, Singh2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-181: But an increase in the thermal conductivity below 352 K (the Debye temperature of germanene) may increase the exponent $\alpha$ ($\sim$2 from the classical calculations) in the scaling relation $\kappa\sim\frac{1 {T^{\alpha }$ below the transition temperature, whereas the exponent will remain the same in the higher temperature regime, which will still lead to the anomalous transition, as evident from our calculations.}}
elements.sent-2411-183: To investigate the underlying heat transport mechanisms in germanene, we analyze the nature of the heat current autocorrelation function (HCACF) through double-exponential fitting: \begin{equation}\frac{\langle \bm{S}(0) \cdot \bm{S}(t) \rangle}{\langle \bm{S}(0) \cdot \bm{S}(0) \rangle} = \alpha_0 e^{-t/\tau_1} + (1 - \alpha_0) e^{-t/\tau_2},\end{equation} where $\tau_1$ is the short range characteristic timescale, $\tau_2$ is the long range characteristic timescale, and $\alpha_0$ (0 $\le \alpha_0 \le$ 1) is a fitting parameter.
elements.sent-2411-184: The short-range characteristic timescale ($\tau_1$) is typically attributed to the high-frequency (optical) phonons \cite{Grujicic2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-185: The initial rapid decay in the HCACF is thus linked to the loss of correlation among these optical phonons.
elements.sent-2411-186: The long-range characteristic timescale ($\tau_2$) is associated with low-frequency (acoustic) phonons.
elements.sent-2411-187: The slow, long-time tail of the HCACF reflects the persistence of correlations among these acoustic phonons, which dominate the contribution to the total thermal conductivity \cite{Grujicic2005, Tretiakov2004, CHEN2010 Fig. \ref{fig:tauvsT (a) shows the temperature dependence of $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-188: A power-law fit yields $\tau_2 \sim T^{-2.04}$ at lower temperatures below $T_c$, closely mirroring the thermal conductivity behavior and $\tau_2 \sim T^{-0.57}$ at higher temperatures above $T_c$.
elements.sent-2411-189: While the double-exponential model elucidates a simplistic connection between thermal conductivity and HCACF decay, mode-dependent phonon analysis is required for a more comprehensive understanding.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[height=6.5cm,keepaspectratio]{fig_4} \caption{\label{fig:dispersion} (a) Phonon dispersion and density of states for germanene along $\Gamma\rightarrow \text{K} \rightarrow \text{M}\rightarrow\Gamma$ direction. The black arrow shows the acoustic-optical gap in the phonon dispersion. (b-d) Angular frequency deviation from harmonic approximation with temperature at high-symmetry $\Gamma$, M, and K points. Notably, the acoustic-optical gap narrows with temperature, contributing to thermal conductivity reduction. The dotted lines represent the transition temperature.}
elements.sent-2411-190: As expected for 2D materials, the ZA mode exhibits quadratic dispersion, while the TA and LA modes show cubic behavior \cite{sapta_beryllene, Gu2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-191: In germanene, the acoustic-optical (AO) gap is the gap between the maximum phonon frequency of the LA mode at the K point and the optical ZO mode at the $\Gamma$-point, as evident from Fig. \ref{fig:dispersion (a).}
elements.sent-2411-192: At $\Gamma$ (Fig. \ref{fig:dispersion (b)), acoustic modes remain unchanged due to symmetry, though the ZO mode shows significant redshift, which in turn reduces the AO gap to a point of overlap between the acoustic and optical phonon branches.}
elements.sent-2411-193: At the M point (Fig. \ref{fig:dispersion (c)), the ZA and TA modes experience an abrupt decrease in frequency around the transition temperature (350 K), with the TA mode exhibiting particularly strong redshifting.}
elements.sent-2411-194: While ZO modes show frequency reductions across temperatures, a minimal shift in LA modes is observed.
elements.sent-2411-195: At the K point, (Fig. \ref{fig:dispersion (d)), the ZA modes show significant redshifting near the transition temperature, while the ZO/TO and LA modes are slightly decreased, leading to a decreased AO gap, and an overlap between the acoustic and optical branches at the transition point.}
elements.sent-2411-196: In the case of the acoustic phonons, two scattering processes are applicable: (i) AAA, signifying the scattering between three acoustic phonons, and (ii) AAO, where two acoustic phonons are absorbed into one optical phonon and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-197: A large AO gap weakens the AAO scattering channel, and in some cases, they might be prohibited \cite{Lindsay2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-198: As a result, a reduction in AO gap due to the increase in temperature leads to a stronger AAO scattering channel and a decreased thermal conductivity \cite{Lindsay2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-199: The sharp AO gap reduction resulting from the significant redshifting of the optical ZO branch at the $\Gamma$-point likely correlates with the anomalous thermal conductivity transition.
elements.sent-2411-200: Moreover, from the phonon density of states calculation (Fig. S5 in SI), it is evident that the AO gap decreases with temperature.
elements.sent-2411-201: An overlap between the acoustic and optical phonon occurs when the temperature is raised to 350 K.
elements.sent-2411-202: The overlap increases with a further increase in the temperature, correlating with the transition.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[height=6.5cm,keepaspectratio]{fig_5} \caption{\label{fig:linewidth} (a) Mode-specific phonon linewidth of germanene at room temperature as a function of angular frequency. Phonon linewidth variation with temperature at (b) $\Gamma$, (c) M, and (d) K points. Linewidth increases with temperature, reducing phonon lifetimes and thermal conductivity. The dotted lines represent the transition temperature.}
elements.sent-2411-203: In Fig. \ref{fig:linewidth (a), we present the linewidth of different phonon branches with phonon angular frequency at room temperature.}
elements.sent-2411-204: The acoustic branches, primarily responsible for thermal transport, are found to possess lower linewidths than optical modes.
elements.sent-2411-205: Moreover, we plot long-wavelength acoustic phonons for a few critical temperatures in Fig. S6 in the SI.
elements.sent-2411-206: Further, in Fig. S7, S8, S9, we plot the phonon linewidth of different phonon branches at the $\Gamma$, M, and K points separately for clarity.
elements.sent-2411-207: We find that the phonon linewidths increase with temperature, reflecting intensified phonon scattering at elevated temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-208: At $\Gamma$-point, optical phonon linewidths, particularly ZO, change significantly near 350 K (Fig. \ref{fig:linewidth (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-209: The acoustic modes show a significant increase in linewidth at the M point.
elements.sent-2411-210: From Fig. \ref{fig:linewidth (c), it is evident that the linewidth of ZA and TA increases significantly at this symmetry point.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Moreover, a clear transition is observed for TA and ZA phonons near the transition temperature, as evident from Fig. S10.
elements.sent-2411-212: This increase in phonon linewidth reduces the phonon lifetime, thus reducing the thermal conductivity significantly.
elements.sent-2411-213: Similarly, at the K point, the ZA/TA acoustic modes show a significant deviation (Fig. S11) in linewidth near 350 K (Fig. \ref{fig:linewidth (d)).}
elements.sent-2411-214: This pronounced followed by a steady increase of acoustic phonon linewidths underpin the observed thermal conductivity transition.
elements.sent-2411-215: In the transition regime, both the lattice constant and buckling decrease sharply, which correlates with a direct decrease in the thermal conductivity.
elements.sent-2411-216: A decrease in the buckling height actually increases the contribution of ZA phonons while reducing the contribution from high-frequency LA phonons \cite{Peng2016prb .}
elements.sent-2411-217: Typically, the flattening of buckling increases the thermal conductivity, whereas the decrease in the lattice constant leads to a decrease in the thermal conductivity \cite{Peng2016prb, Keshri2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-218: Based on the variation of thermal conductivity with temperature, it is likely that among these contradictory effects, the decrease in the conductivity outplays the effect of a possible increase in the conductivity due to reduced buckling.
elements.sent-2411-219: In low-dimensional materials, the thermal conductivity often exhibits a pronounced dependence on the sample dimensions, challenging the assumption of intrinsic behavior.
elements.sent-2411-220: Nonequilibrium-based approach (Eq. \ref{eqn:fourier ) is suitable for understanding the length dependence of thermal conductivity as the approach can simulate a microdevice measurement on a sample placed between hot and cold regions (Fig. S4(a) in SI) \cite{Gu2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-221: This is in agreement with the thermal conductivity of germanene nanoribbons, calculated previously (Fig. S4(b)) \cite{Peng2016prb .}
elements.sent-2411-222: Here, the scaled HCACF exhibits a decay of $t^{-1.6}$ (Fig. S12 in SI), indicating convergent thermal conductivity.
elements.sent-2411-223: Consequently, the ZA phonons with similar lifetime (shown in Fig. S13(a)) in SI contribute less to the overall thermal conductivity (only $\sim$33\% to total conductivity, compared to $\sim$ 76\% in graphene \cite{Peng2016prb, Fan2017prb ), leading to convergence at larger sample sizes.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Further, large wavelength ZA phonons show $\omega \sim q^{2}$ scaling (Fig. S13(b) in SI), which is consistent with convergent thermal conductivity \cite{Gu2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-225: Our analysis reveals that the thermal conductivity follows an unusual scaling: $\kappa \sim T^{-2}$ below $T_c$, transitioning to $\kappa \sim T^{-1/2}$ above $T_c$, a stark departure from the typical $\kappa \sim T^{-1}$ trend.
elements.sent-2411-226: Phonon analysis further elucidates this behavior, with a significant reduction in the AO gap, primarily due to redshifting of the ZO phonon modes at high-symmetry $\Gamma$ point.
elements.sent-2411-227: This redshifting, coupled with a significant deviation in acoustic phonon linewidths near $T_c$, signifies intensified phonon scattering, fundamentally altering the thermal transport pathways.
